responses.strategic_rationale: The core tensions center on achieving high-fidelity, longitudinal parasomnia data within a safe, sustainable residential research setting while preserving participant privacy and enabling scalable analytics. Priorities include: (a) data richness through tiered sensing and staged PSG, (b) governance that protects participants and enables selective data use, (c) efficient enrollment and retention of adults 18–65 with NREM parasomnias, (d) a staffing model that sustains 8 active suites with safety margins, (e) robust yet extensible technology infrastructure, and (f) ethically sound privacy controls that do not unduly constrain research velocity. The 6 levers address these axes and introduce radical options that leverage emerging tech and inventive business models to unlock scale if validated. A missing translational/regulatory plan could impede clinical deployment beyond Bonn; the levers anticipate this risk via scalable data sharing and monetization pathways tied to ethics and governance.

levers.name: Longitudinal residential monitoring fidelity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Sensor calibration and data capture protocols tighten to improve detection of infrequent parasomnia events. → Systemic: Event yield increases by 15% as measured by adjudicated episodes per participant, but false alarm rate rises by 5% requiring additional review capacity. → Strategic: Higher data quality accelerates phenotype characterization but increases staffing and operational costs, challenging the plan’s cost ceiling.
options: Maintain the current nightly monitoring cadence with incremental calibration of sensors to improve event detection without increasing scope.
Adopt a data-driven triage rule to escalate to enhanced PSG only on pre-defined triggers derived from sensor fusion and preliminary annotations.
Launch a cloud-enabled, modular full-night PSG platform on-demand with a data-as-a-service licensing model for external researchers.
levers.review_lever: Controls data fidelity vs. cost containment. Weakness: The options fail to consider the transition costs and regulatory review timelines required to scale data-sharing across platforms.

levers.name: Data governance and access architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Tiered data access and dynamic consent reduce privacy risk and streamline internal approvals. → Systemic: Time-to-data-use approvals decreases by 40%, but broader external collaboration options shrink by 25%, constraining rapid cross-center validation. → Strategic: Governance framework sustains ethical integrity while balancing openness with monetization potential.
options: Maintain existing de-identification and data-sharing constraints while ensuring compliance.
Implement tiered data access with dynamic consent and granular data-use agreements to balance openness and privacy.
Launch a federated analytics framework with secure enclaves and a paid research collaboration model that monetizes access to anonymized datasets while preserving privacy.
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy vs openness. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability challenges and governance overhead across multiple partner institutions.

levers.name: Recruitment and enrollment optimization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Structured pre-admission screening improves enrollment efficiency by reducing screen-fail rates. → Systemic: Enrollment yield rises by 12% but recruitment costs increase by 18% per participant, affecting cost efficiency. → Strategic: The unit attains higher sample diversity and statistical power at greater unit cost, requiring budget reallocation or fundraising.
options: Continue existing recruitment channels with modest regional expansion.
Introduce structured pre-admission screening clinics and formal referrals to improve yield.
Establish a tele-recruitment platform with performance-based incentives and predictive screening to accelerate enrollment.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment channels vs cost. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant burden and consent complexity across platforms.

levers.name: Staffing, safety, and governance framework
levers.consequences: Immediate: Rotating night coverage with cross-training reduces fatigue-related errors and improves response times. → Systemic: Staffing costs rise by 12% and coverage reliability improves by 8 percentage points, trading off budget pressure for safety and throughput. → Strategic: The improved safety and throughput justify higher personnel costs but necessitate ongoing funding and efficiency measures.
options: Maintain current staffing with minor SOP refinements.
Implement rotating night coverage with cross-training and standardized SOPs.
Adopt a flexible, on-demand staffing marketplace with performance-based payments and strict safety rails.
levers.review_lever: Controls staffing flexibility vs cost containment. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory constraints on shift patterns and recruitment pipelines.

levers.name: Technological platform and data infrastructure
levers.consequences: Immediate: Modular sensor suite and synchronized data streams improve interoperability and reduce downtime. → Systemic: Data processing throughput increases by 30% with a 10% capex increase, trading speed for cost. → Strategic: Enables scalable, shareable analytics while requiring longer-term capital commitments.
options: Continue current modular sensor stack and data pipeline with incremental upgrades.
Enhance automated quality-control pipelines and real-time annotation prompts.
Create an open ecosystem data platform with containerized analysis pipelines and a data-licensing model for external researchers.
levers.review_lever: Controls throughput vs capital expenditure. Weakness: The options fail to consider vendor lock-in risks and data standardization across partners.

levers.name: Ethics, safety, and privacy controls
levers.consequences: Immediate: Privacy-preserving measures reduce risk and facilitate consent processing. → Systemic: A privacy-by-design approach lowers breach probability by 40% but may constrain some data-sharing capabilities by 10-20%, trading openness for safety. → Strategic: Strengthens trust and regulatory alignment at potential cost to external collaborations.
options: Maintain current ethics approvals with routine audits.
Implement continuous risk monitoring and enhanced on-site safety drills.
Adopt privacy-by-design with differential privacy techniques, automated consent management, and a live data-sharing marketplace.
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy vs openness. Weakness: The options fail to consider ongoing governance for consent withdrawal and cross-border data transfers.

responses.summary: The levers cover data fidelity, governance, recruitment, staffing, platform infrastructure, and privacy controls, aligning with the Bonn-based parasomnia study’s risk-reward balance. A critical missing dimension is translational/regulatory deployment planning and multi-site scalability; Add '[Launch a phased regulatory and translational plan with industry partnerships to scale and deploy the event-triage platform across clinical networks]' to Lever 5.

responses.strategic_rationale: The core tensions for establishing a Bonn residential parasomnia unit revolve around ecological validity vs safety and privacy, data fidelity vs budget, and throughput vs human review quality. The proposed levers push the project toward higher data integrity and generalizable phenotyping while acknowledging escalating costs, staffing demands, and governance complexity. By sequencing conservative, moderate, and radical options, we test whether precision-oriented protocols can scale within the 3-year budget while maintaining patient safety and ethical integrity. The levers also probe the trade-off between centralized versus decentralized data handling, ensuring that any radical data-sharing approaches preserve participant trust and regulatory compliance. These choices aim to maximize publishable insights and grant-readiness without prematurely commodifying the research platform.

levers.name: Naturalistic residence fidelity optimization
levers.consequences: Immediate: The residence adopts a more naturalistic environment → Systemic: Data completeness improves from 78% to 86% and usable nights rise by about 1 per participant; however, renovation and ongoing costs increase by ~12% and staffing needs grow ~15% (trade-off: ecological validity vs budget and throughput) → Strategic: The study yields more ecologically valid phenotypes and stronger publication potential. Operational constraints require revised budgeting. This slows ramp-up but enhances external credibility for grant applications.
options: Enhance safety and comfort features while maintaining current surveillance intensity to protect privacy and minimize disruption
Introduce structured naturalistic observation modules with predefined escalation criteria to preserve data fidelity while controlling risk
Adopt edge-computing enabled, privacy-preserving on-device scoring with a data-cooperative governance model to share de-identified insights with external researchers
levers.review_lever: Controls naturalistic environment fidelity vs. participant privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider transition costs.

levers.name: Tiered PSG escalation protocol
levers.consequences: Immediate: Tiered PSG escalation protocol is implemented with predefined triggers → Systemic: Average nights per participant increase from 1.8 to 2.4 and escalation costs rise ~10%, while diagnostic yield improves by 6 percentage points (trade-off: thoroughness vs staffing burden) → Strategic: The protocol enhances data richness and result validity but requires greater budget and personnel planning. This may constrain enrollment pace if budgets are fixed. A cross-institution collaboration could mitigate costs.
options: Maintain nightly low-burden PSG monitoring with fixed escalation triggers to minimize disruption
Integrate automated flag-based escalations for probable events and reserve additional PSG nights for high-yield cases
Establish universal escalation via mobile PSG units and cloud-based central scoring with cross-institution data exchange
levers.review_lever: Controls escalation thoroughness vs. staffing costs. Weakness: The options fail to consider staffing constraints.

levers.name: Semi-automated event triage workflow
levers.consequences: Immediate: Semi-automated triage workflow is adopted with rule-based pre-filtering → Systemic: Time to adjudicate events decreases from 12 minutes to 8 minutes per event and inter-rater reliability improves from kappa 0.78 to 0.86, but false positives risk increases by ~1.5 percentage points (trade-off: throughput vs accuracy) → Strategic: The workflow elevates throughput and consistency while preserving human oversight. This requires ongoing QA and calibration.
options: Maintain dual human scoring with expanded guidelines to improve reliability
Implement rule-based triage pre-filtering to prioritize high-probability events for manual review
Deploy machine-augmented triage with real-time cross-scoring and secure external validation via a data portal
levers.review_lever: Controls triage automation efficiency vs. human review accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider false-positive rate and missed events.

levers.name: Participant retention and engagement scaffolding
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participant engagement scaffolding is introduced to maintain enthusiasm → Systemic: Enrollment conversion rises from 40% to 58% and dropout rates drop by 15%, but staff time for engagement increases ~20% (trade-off: participant burden vs data completeness) → Strategic: Improved retention yields richer longitudinal data and more robust analyses. Operational implications include staffing reallocation and schedule flexibility. This strategy strengthens study power but raises ongoing labor costs.
options: Maintain current compensation and scheduling to minimize burden
Introduce tiered engagement with flexible visit windows, caregiver involvement, and feedback loops
Co-create participant governance with dashboards, optional extended on-site lodging, and involvement in study design
levers.review_lever: Controls participant engagement vs staff burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider caregiver capacity.

levers.name: Cross-site data harmonization and EEG-BIDS standardization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Cross-site harmonization initiatives begin → Systemic: Data latency reduces from ~28 days to ~14 days and interoperability improves by ~40%, but governance overhead increases (trade-off: interoperability vs governance overhead) → Strategic: Enables robust multi-site phenotyping and external collaboration. This will require formal data-use agreements and ongoing coordination across sites.
options: Implement local data schemas aligned with Bonn protocols without cross-site changes
Establish EEG-BIDS compatibility and shared metadata templates across Bonn and partner centers
Create a cross-institution data-sharing consortium with standardized pipelines, secure de-identification, and open-access de-identified datasets via a research portal
levers.review_lever: Controls cross-site harmonization vs. data latency. Weakness: The options fail to consider interoperability across legacy systems.

levers.name: Privacy-preserving video handling and de-identification pipeline
levers.consequences: Immediate: Privacy-preserving controls for video streams are strengthened → Systemic: Access requests processing time reduces from 7 days to 3 days and de-identification success rate increases to 99.5%; however processing costs rise ~6% (trade-off: privacy vs data utility) → Strategic: Maintains participant trust while enabling broader data use and collaboration. This requires robust edge processing and governance for external access.
options: Maintain strict on-storage de-identification and role-based access controls
Implement automated anonymization with audit trails and consent-driven access revocation
Develop edge-processing video analytics and secure multi-party computation to enable external collaboration without exposing raw footage
levers.review_lever: Controls video privacy vs data utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider feasibility of edge processing.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-institution governance and external validation for data sharing, which would enable robust external benchmarking while preserving participant privacy. Add 'Establish a cross-institution data-sharing consortium with standardized EEG-BIDS pipelines and a controlled-access de-identified dataset portal' to Cross-site data harmonization and EEG-BIDS standardization

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn residential parasomnia unit must simultaneously ensure participant safety, maximize physiologic data quality, and enable scalable analytics to deliver robust phenotyping and publishable methods validation. Core tensions include data richness vs. staffing and costs, privacy/regulatory constraints vs. cross-site collaboration, participant incentives vs. budget stability, and expansion velocity vs. governance burden. The six levers introduce a spectrum of conservative to radical options that progressively reallocate resources, adopt privacy-preserving data ecosystems, and experiment with modular facilities and dynamic consent. Together they reduce single-site bias, accelerate knowledge generation, and set the stage for phase-two intervention studies while maintaining ethical rigor and operational resilience.

levers.name: Parasomnia capture protocol optimization
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standardizing nightly low-burden monitoring increases the probability of capturing at least one parasomnia event per participant on a typical stay by 15%. Systemic: Event yield rises by 25%, but annotation workload increases by 12% and average stay duration extends by 0.5–1 day, trading data richness for higher staffing and cost. Strategic: Improves longitudinal phenotyping power and generalizability, but requires budget reallocation and enhanced governance for data volume.
options: Standardize nightly low-burden monitoring across all participants to maximize data comparability.
Incorporate adaptive sampling with selective enhanced nights based on interim yield to balance data richness with participant burden.
Launch federated, privacy-preserving edge-processing with cross-site data-sharing consortium to scale phenotyping without central raw data transfer.
levers.review_lever: Controls data capture depth vs. staffing burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider cost escalation and scheduling bottlenecks.

levers.name: Cross-site privacy-preserving data ecosystem design
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enables cross-site analyses without transferring identifiable data. Systemic: Decreases data transfer by 70% and increases collaboration breadth by 60%, but adds governance overhead and consent management costs of 25%. Strategic: Broadens external validity and funding potential, but slows decision cycles due to governance processes.
options: Limit data sharing to de-identified aggregated statistics with local processing.
Establish standardized metadata and secure transfer protocols to enable controlled cross-site analyses.
Create a data-services platform funded by a multi-stakeholder data-commons model, offering tiered access to de-identified datasets and on-demand analytics.
levers.review_lever: Controls data access vs. privacy and safety compliance. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory alignment and dynamic consent overhead.

levers.name: Participant engagement economics
levers.consequences: Immediate: Incentives boost enrollment by 20% over six months. Systemic: Per-participant cost declines 10% due to improved retention, but total administrative overhead rises 15%. Strategic: Accelerates enrollment velocity and supports broader recruitment, but requires robust grant management and governance oversight.
options: Maintain existing per-night compensation and meals with fixed schedule.
Introduce tiered incentives and flexible visit timing to boost enrollment while monitoring budget.
Adopt pay-for-performance model with philanthropic micro-grants to accelerate recruitment and offer co-authorship opportunities.
levers.review_lever: Controls incentives vs. budget stability. Weakness: The options fail to consider donor dependency risk and long-term sustainability.

levers.name: Phased modular facility expansion
levers.consequences: Immediate: Expansion readiness reduces downtime during reconfiguration by 2 weeks. Systemic: Capacity increases to 12 suites; occupancy utilization improves by 25%, but upfront capex up 40% and financing complexity. Strategic: Enables scalable expansion and cross-site replication, but raises governance complexity and project risk.
options: Maintain 8-suite footprint with safety upgrades and no structural changes.
Phase in 12-suite capacity using modular walls and gating, contingent on data quality.
Deploy fully modular suite pods with telepresence-enabled operations and remote facility management.
levers.review_lever: Controls expansion speed vs. governance complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider long lead times for regulatory approvals and safety certifications.

levers.name: Dynamic consent and ethics governance
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participants can adjust consent preferences in real time. Systemic: Compliance agility improves by 40%, but requires new software and governance overhead rising 20%. Strategic: Improves trust and flexibility for adaptive protocols, but increases ongoing operational complexity.
options: Maintain current consent processes and annual ethics reviews.
Implement periodic re-consent checkpoints and privacy risk dashboards.
Implement dynamic consent platform with real-time preference controls and a cross-institution ethics charter.
levers.review_lever: Controls consent flexibility vs. administrative overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider technological maintenance burden and user-interface usability.

levers.name: Advanced event triage data-in-motion
levers.consequences: Immediate: Triage speed improves by 25%, reducing manual review time. Systemic: Throughput improves 30%, but sync overhead and training require 15% more staff time. Strategic: Accelerates publication and method validation while increasing IT governance needs.
options: Maintain dual human scoring with standard workflow.
Introduce semi-automated triage with probabilistic tagging to prioritize reviews.
Deploy privacy-preserving cross-site federated triage engine with edge processing and clinician-in-the-loop validation.
levers.review_lever: Controls annotation throughput vs. cross-site data privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider integration with existing scoring criteria and data-label drift.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is explicit regulatory alignment for cross-site data sharing and dynamic consent, including GDPR-compliant consent management and cross-institution ethics governance. Add 'data-services platform with multi-stakeholder funding and tiered access to de-identified datasets, governed by a cross-institution charter' to Cross-site privacy-preserving data ecosystem design

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences, in this order):
     "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]. Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
     Both clauses are mandatory in every response, in this exact order, in one field.
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

2026-Mar-11

Project start ASAP
